Does let Get Hoisted? Meet the Temporal Dead Zone (JS Core #61)

Everyone knows var gets hoisted — but what about let? This post digs into whether let is hoisted too, why calling it early throws a ReferenceError instead of giving you undefined, and what the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) actually is. We'll also use a typeof trick to prove let really does get hoisted, just in a different way.

CodeSandbox is genuinely great — and pretty different from CodePen. Where CodePen is "online editor for snippets", CodeSandbox is more like "your local dev environment, in the cloud" — full templates for React / Vue / Angular / Node / static, real npm install support, and a UI that's 87% VSCode. This post walks through the basics: creating a sandbox, installing packages, keyboard shortcuts, and exporting back to a local project.
What Does var a, b = (1, 2) Actually Output? (And Why)

A fun little JavaScript brain-teaser was making the rounds on my Facebook feed, and it happened to come up in our work Q&A channel too. So I'm writing it down. We break down var a, b = (1, 2) piece by piece — multiple variable declaration, the grouping operator, and the comma operator — to figure out exactly why a is undefined and b is 2.
JavaScript Core (60) - ES6: let & const - Practical Tricks for let and const

Last chapter covered the basics of let and const. This time we get into some real-world stuff — the classic interview loop-with-setTimeout gotcha, why let fixes it, the closure hiding inside it, and what const actually locks down when it points at an object (plus how Object.freeze and 'use strict' fit in).
